figures.caption: Table 2: Evaluation on Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B [21] and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-1.5B [3]. Baseline results from [23]. Bold indicates best among merging methods. Avg. Length is average response length in tokens across all benchmarks. FIM-TIES results are averaged over 4 random seeds (std < 0.3 on all benchmarks).

Figure 3: Accuracy vs. average response length trade-off on 1.5B L2S models. FIM-TIES (ours) achieves the highest accuracy (47.3%) with the shortest response length (411 tokens), simultaneously dominating all baselines on both dimensions. Baseline lengths computed from [23] Table 2.
Table 3: Evaluation on Qwen2.5-Math-7B [21] and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-7B [3]. Baseline results from [23]. Bold indicates best among merging methods under greedy decoding.

Figure 4: Nonlinearity analysis of Long-to-Short model merging (Qwen2.5→ Qwen2.5-Math at 1.5B & 7B scales). Left: Per-layer NL Score (at α = 0.5) decreases monotonically from early to late layers (1.5B mean = 0.240, 7B mean = 0.171). Middle: NL Score vs. relative delta scale (1.5B); submodule linearity holds only at small delta scales (≲ 0.75×). Right: Strong positive correlation between NL Score and relative merging error (7B; Pearson r = 0.972, p < 10−17), empirically supporting Proposition 1.

Figure 1: Overall framework of FIM-Merging. Given a base model θ0 and a fine-tuned model θ1, FIM-Merging computes diagonal FIM on θ0 using N = 8 random token inputs (no calibration data required) and estimates per-layer task vector norms ∥δl∥2. Their product F̂ l·∥δl∥2 directly instantiates the Hessian bound in Proposition 1, and is used to assign layer-adaptive merging coefficients αl via log-space normalization and sigmoid mapping. Early layers with high FIM scores receive conservative αl, while later layers receive aggressive αl. The resulting coefficients are applied within an enhanced TIES-Merging procedure with gate protection and residual norm calibration to produce the merged model θm.

Figure 2: Per-layer merging coefficients αl assigned by FIM-Merging at 1.5B and 7B scales. Early layers receive lower α (conservative merging) due to higher FIM×∥δ∥2 scores, consistent with Proposition 1. The 7B model shows stronger layer differentiation, reflecting greater variation in per-layer Hessian norm across scales.
